title: HLA-B*57:01 Testing -- Indications
content: Patients with HIV should be screened for HLA-B*57:01 when preparing an antiretroviral therapy drug regimen, especially when considering abacavir. Even if previously tolerated, screening should happen before restarting abacavir therapy if the HLA-B*57:01 status is unknown. Implementing HLA-B*57:01 screening in clinical practice has been shown to reduce the incidence of abacavir hypersensitivity reactions from 1.3% to 0.2% over several years, demonstrating the clinical benefit of genetic screening before treatment initiation. [17] [8]

title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Clinical Significance
content: A campaign named “Choosing Wisely” by the American Board of Internal Medicine in North America advocates for choosing clinical value tests and encourages patients and healthcare workers to mobilize laboratory resources wisely. Tests that are ordered sometimes have major side effects: they are responsible for a <5% increase in healthcare costs but affect 70% of major decisions regarding admission, treatment, and discharge plan. A study defined by clinical guideline’s recommendations demonstrated that repeat testing is unnecessary in about 25 to 30% of cases. Among medical specialties, family practitioners represent the largest group of doctors ordering laboratory tests, followed by the internal medicine department. [5] [6] [7] [1] [2]

title: HLA-B*57:01 Testing -- Normal and Critical Findings
content: In patients with a positive result, abacavir is contraindicated. Due to the implications of positive results for the HLA-B*57:01 allele, using abacavir in treatment is avoided. Instead, clinicians should substitute a different NRTI in the therapeutic regimen. Conversely, a negative result signifies that the patient does not possess the HLA-B*57:01 allele. If the patient is negative for the allele, clinicians can safely resume abacavir as a component of the patient's regimen. [8]

title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Indications -- Test Selection
content: Simultaneous or sequential testing is not recommended, except in cases where the initial test is negative, indeterminate, or borderline if the clinical suspicion is high. In that case, repeat testing with TST or IGRA (depending on which test was done initially) can be done. Additionally, the TST or IGRA should not be repeated if they were previously positive or to follow therapy, as this positive result is expected to be retained, and differences in results are challenging to interpret. [13]

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2050
content: Laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy; without salpingectomy and/or oophorectomy is a minimally invasive surgical approach that seeks to preserve the fallopian tube and ovary while addressing an abnormal implantation. In the context of this procedure, fetal monitoring is employed as a diagnostic and surveillance tool to evaluate fetal status, even though ectopic pregnancies rarely result in a viable fetus. Fetal monitoring itself is categorized under إجراءات تصوير متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي, which encompasses imaging procedures related to the genital and breast organs. While the concept of “has child” may be linked to fetal monitoring in the abstract, clinical reality dictates that ectopic pregnancies typically do not lead to a live birth; therefore, the expectation of a child should be understood as a theoretical rather than a common outcome. Integrating organ‑preserving laparoscopic surgery with appropriate fetal monitoring and the broader suite of genital‑ and breast‑related imaging modalities supports comprehensive patient care and informed decision‑making.
